title: How Lithuania became a tech startup nation
raw_text:
Cyber City in Lithuania’s capital, Vilnius, was built on the site of a textile factory, which in its day turned out millions of socks a year. Today, you can see reminders of the textile design in the decoration of buildings that house more than 3,000 technology professionals and high-tech businesses.

Since it gained independence from the Soviet Union in 1990, Lithuania, a country of 2.8 million people, has reinvented itself as a home for tech startups. Since 2020, the value of Lithuania’s tech sector has grown by a factor of six to reach €16.4bn. Many of its companies are household names, such as Vinted, the online second-hand fashion market, and cyber security and privacy company Nord Security. 

Lithuania’s technology sector has grown through a mixture of inward investment and homegrown companies. More than 70,000 people work for companies in the ICT sector. It has strengths in fintech, robotics, cyber security, health and climate technology, lasers and enterprise software. Six of the country’s startup companies have reached unicorn status, securing valuations of over $1bn.

For much of its history, venture capital funding has been in short supply for companies growing out of Lithuania. This has bred a culture of self-sufficiency among the country’s entrepreneurs, creating businesses that are careful with their cash and plough their profits back into growth – bootstrapping, in other words.

Many entrepreneurs look to Israel, the original startup nation, for inspiration for growing their own startup culture. Like Israel, Lithuania is a small country with difficult borders, and startup founders have learned from its approach.

Lithuania’s first unicorn

Vinted was one of Lithuania’s early success stories and became the first Lithuanian company to reach unicorn status in 2019.

The company started in 2008 as a website built to help a 21-year-old who wanted to downsize her wardrobe before moving house.

Today, Vinted is well known across the world as an online marketplace for second-hand clothes and fashion items. It employs over 2,000 people.

At least 25 staff from Vinted have gone on to build their own startups, according to Gintarė Verbickaitė, CEO of Unicorns Lithuania, a membership organisation for Lithuanian startups. “We call it the Vinted effect,” she said.

Other online markets have followed Vinted. One is Tingit, an online market that connects people who want to repair clothes, shoes or handbags with expert repairers. Another, Ovoko, helps people find used car parts from scrap yards, and another online market connects pet owners and pet sitters.

The rise of fintech

Fintech is the crown jewel in Lithuania’s startup ecosystem. The fintech sector as a whole grew from 44 companies in 2014 to 250 in 2025 and now serves 40 million people across the European Union (EU), according to figures from Invest Lithuania, a government agency that promotes direct foreign investment in the country.

After the UK’s split from the EU, Lithuania’s Ministry of Finance and the Bank of Lithuania saw an opportunity to build the country’s own financial services sector, which until that point had been dominated by a few Scandinavian banks.

The government responded by introducing a fast-track programme to allow financial services companies to obtain a licence in just six months. That compares to two years for the next fastest EU country, said Verbickaitė.

“We needed more competition in our local financial services market, and we needed better access to financing for our small and medium-sized companies, so it made sense from a government perspective to drive the growth of that sector,” she added.

A turning point came when the challenger bank Revolut established a branch in Vilnius in 2017. The city went on to become the hub of Revolut’s European business following Brexit. Revolut was able to apply for a digital banking licence, which allowed it to operate as a bank without issuing loans before it gained a full licence in 2021.

Other international financial brands followed Revolut – from the UK, the US, Southeast Asia and South Korea. They include companies such as payment service Sumup, which won a licence in 2019, and smart wallet company Curve in 2020. Payment processing company Robinhood received a licence to open a crypto centre in Vilnius in 2025, and Checkout opened a technology centre in Vilnius in 2026.

Fintechs have, in turn, attracted cyber security and IT infrastructure companies to provide support services. Tech services companies with a presence in Lithuania include Cognizant, the Israeli enterprise cloud company TeraSky, and Santa Monica Networks.

One sign of Lithuania’s confidence in the fintech sector is that last year the country bid against Frankfurt and Paris to host Europe’s new Anti Money Laundering Authority (AMLA), although AMLA ultimately went to Frankfurt.

Lithuania’s tech unicorns

1. Vinted

Vinted became Lithuania’s first tech unicorn in 2019. The online market for second-hand clothes and fashion items was originally set up by its co-founder as a way to empty her wardrobe of unwanted clothes before moving house. From its beginnings in Vilnius in 2008, the company has expanded across Europe and the US and is now valued at €8bn.

2. Nord Security

Nord Security was founded by two childhood friends who launched an online privacy tool, NordVPN, in Vilnius in 2012. The company grew, reinvesting its revenues to develop new products launched under the Nord name, which were brought under the umbrella of Nord Security in 2020. Two years later, it became Lithuania’s second tech unicorn, and by 2023 was valued at €3bn.

3. Baltic Classifieds Group (BCG)

BCG operates online classified advertising portals in the Baltic states, for cars, real estate, jobs, services and other goods. Founded in 1999, the company became Lithuania’s third unicorn in 2023. It is listed on the London Stock Exchange.

4. Flow

Flow, described as a femtech company, was founded in Belarus in 2015 to offer a hormone and health tracking app for women. It set up a hub in Lithuania in 2020, which employed more than half of the company’s global engineering workforce. It reached unicorn status in 2024 and is claimed by Lithuania as one of its success stories because of its significant presence in the country.

5. Cast AI

Cast AI describes itself as a cloud optimisation platform. The company started life in Florida, but added a hub in Lithuania, which now develops most of Cast AI’s software, serves customers, manages projects and handles marketing. It achieved unicorn status in 2026. Its customers include BMW, pharmaceutical and technology companies.

6. Oxylabs

Oxylabs is a “web intelligence” platform that uses ethically sourced consumer IP addresses to collect data from the public internet. The company, founded in 2015, secured a $130m investment from Warburg Pincus, valuing it at $3.6m. Oxylabs sees a big future in using its infrastructure to provide and retrieve data for AI models, AI agents and chatbots.

The bootstrapping nation

Lithuania’s business community had to learn from scratch when the country broke away from the Soviet Union in 1990.

“Thirty-five years ago, you could go to prison if you started your own business,” said Verbickaitė. “So this whole entrepreneurship mentality is actually quite fresh.”

“Thirty-five years ago, you could go to prison if you started your own business, so this whole entrepreneurship mentality is actually quite fresh”

Gintarė Verbickaitė, Unicorns Lithuania

The country had to rebuild its market economy, and there was little access to venture capital to set up new companies.

That has led to a disciplined focus on generating revenue and growing by ploughing profits back into the business. “That has really helped shape the mentality to be focused on operational efficiency, and on financial discipline,” she said.

The importance of Lithuania’s business to the country is reflected in both government policy and popular culture. The government, for example, has invested in a national reskilling programme, which has trained more than 20,000 people in digital skills since 2022.

Young people can legally register an educational company, attract funding, appoint shareholders and a CEO to make revenues while they learn how to run a business, said Verbickaitė. They can also take part in a Lithuanian equivalent of Dragon’s Den for young people, known as Shark Tank.

Nord Security

Laurynas Zabulis is the group chief financial officer (CFO) at Nord Security, a privacy and cyber security company based in Cyber City that provides services to consumers and small businesses – and another of Lithuania’s success stories.

“I think the mentality that was sort of instilled from our bootstrapping days is very much intact and is still evident right now,” he said.

“I think the mentality that was sort of instilled from our bootstrapping days is very much intact and is still evident right now”

Laurynas Zabulis, Nord Security

These days, the company has predictable revenue from subscribers who renew their subscriptions, and can be more aggressive about using that to fund new ventures.

But he said the company is still “prudent” in how it allocates capital and measures the return on investment for every transaction. “We still have that ethos ingrained within the company.”

When Nord raised its first external investment in 2022, valuing the company at $1.6bn, it was not because the company lacked cash flow or needed to fill gaps in its budget.

“It gives you more credibility if you have raised external capital, particularly at a high valuation,” he said. “It also provided us with more flexibility and more open doors, particularly when it came to hiring talent from overseas.”

The company has been able to open offices in Germany and Poland, put money back into research and development, and has invested funds as a financial buffer.

For Zabulis, making money is part of the national character. Even during the Soviet times, Lithuanians looked for ways they could make money on the side, he said, describing them as having a kind of “entrepreneurial grit”.

Nord Security was one of the first companies to raise capital funding from tier one investors. The company had to work out for itself how to approach investment banks, how to negotiate legal contracts, and which legal firms to hire.

Nord has shared its knowledge with other startups and support organisations such as Unicorns Lithuania, and Nord’s experience has become “institutional knowledge”.

Zabulis is a member of chat groups with other CFOs from large tech companies who help each other out when problems arise. “We have a market that kind of cultivates this knowledge sharing,” he said.

Freedom to take risks

Hostinger, a web hosting and cloud infrastructure company, was founded in 2004 and remained a small IT company based in Lithuania with a staff of 20 or 30 people until it started growing rapidly during Covid-19.

The company has resisted venture capital funding, preferring instead to fund growth through its own revenues. This has given the company greater freedom to move quickly and take risks that institutional investors may not approve of.

“External investors mean more voices at the board table, and slower processes sometimes, so while we can do it, we like financing our own business,” said spokesperson Eiviltas Paraščiakas.

For example, the company took a bet on opening in India, going up against intense competition. The bet paid off, and now India is a big market for Hostinger.

Would the company have won backing from venture capital companies to make such a move? Paraščiakas has his doubts. “It could look like a risky move back then,” he said.

From datacentres to AI

Alongside its homegrown startups, Lithuania is marketing itself as a destination for high-tech companies.

Priority areas include datacentres, clean tech, defence, business services and advanced manufacturing.

The country hosts a small datacentre for Swedish telecoms provider Telia and local companies, and Telia has others under construction. The country is also holding talks with some global Fortune 500 companies to build larger centres.

Over 80% of the country’s power comes from renewable energy, and work is underway with other Baltic states to build an offshore wind turbine in the Baltic Sea, which could supply power to one or two large datacentres.

The country has several sites available with pre-made infrastructure, including one with 100 megawatts of power, enough for a hyperscale datacentre, some 80km outside of Lithuania’s second city, Kaunas.

The country is building a €130m artificial intelligence (AI) centre, LitAI, that aims to become a national centre for AI. Work is also underway to develop a native-language Lithuanian large language model.

Lithuania’s sixth unicorn

Another company on the Cyber City campus, Oxylabs, became Lithuania’s sixth unicorn this month after securing £130m investment from Warburg Pincus, valuing the company at $3.6bn.

Oxylabs began life in 2015 as a web scraping service, initially collecting real-time data on flight ticket pricing from multiple websites, using proxy IP addresses to avoid having its traffic blocked, its CEO Vytautas Savickas told Computer Weekly.

Like other companies in Cyber City, Oxylabs benefited from support from Tesonet, the startup accelerator that developed Cyber City. Its founders are still active in the company. “They are still in the operational parts, helping us, pushing us,” said Savickas.

“Lithuanians are used to rejection, we are used to people saying no, and we are good at working through it. We have this small nation, and we need to be loud to be heard”

Vytautas Savickas, Oxylabs

The company works with Fortune 500 companies to monitor cyber security threats, counterfeit websites and pricing information. Savickas said he is prevented by confidentiality agreements from disclosing names, but they include big e-commerce sites, large tech companies and artificial intelligence (AI) labs.

The company’s infrastructure allows its customers to collect real-time data from public websites and feed that data into their own IT systems to make decisions, improve their products, or gather intelligence.

“There are a lot of new chatbots popping up in websites. If the customer connects their chatbots to our infrastructure, they can retrieve information …and get the most accurate answer for the customer,” he said.

The company works with Honeygain, a service that allows people to earn a passive income by making a proportion of their bandwidth available to Oxylabs to use, allowing Oxylabs to scrape data from multiple IP addresses, without being blocked.

CEO Vytautas Savickas said that because Lithuania is a small market, startup companies like Oxylabs have to think about selling to bigger markets, such as the US, from day one.

They also have to be very persistent, he said, joking that if a Lithuanian sees a locked door, they will bang their head against it until it opens. “We are used to rejection, we are used to people saying no, and we are good at working through it. We have this small nation, and we need to be loud to be heard,” he added.

Following its injection of cash from Warburg Pincus, Oxylabs is gearing up to expand overseas and invest in new products.

“It’s a signal for us that we are going in the right direction, going with an external investor and going through rigorous due diligence,” he said. “It really shows that we have good internal operations, that our books and risk compliance work is of a high standard.”

Nevertheless, he said there are a lot of successful companies in Lithuania that are doing well without external investment. “We don’t create hype, we create results,” he said.

More unicorns on the way

As the country expands its technology infrastructure, it is confident of spinning out more high-tech startups.

Verbickaitė is confident that the country will claim 10 unicorn-sized startups by 2029. There are already companies waiting in the wings that are approaching the right scale.

And AI is making it possible for companies to grow much faster than before. Small teams can design and build products using AI in a much shorter time, she said.

“We have already seen globally, and even in Europe, startups that could become unicorns in less than a year or less than two years,” she added.

Startups on the front line 

With Lithuania bordering the Russian enclave of Kaliningrad to the west, Belarus to the south-east, and St Petersburg less than 1,000km away, national security is a consideration for companies in Vilnius.

When Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022, many employees in Lithuania’s startup companies gave up their weekends and evenings to raise money or provide help and resources to Ukraine.

Eiviltas Paraščiakas, head of communications at Hostinger, co-led one such project. He recalls receiving a call from Kyiv while travelling by car from Lithuania’s second city, Kaunas. He pulled into a petrol station for a briefing on how to make drones.

It sounded simple, until he returned to the office and found he would have to order the parts from over two dozen different suppliers.

“We started organising these workshops, and it was a great success,” he told Computer Weekly. “We were on TV because I think we were among the first companies doing that. We had a lot of workshops during the weekends, and our colleagues were coming here with families, with kids, with parents, and we had a lot of boxes with drone parts, and colleagues were just making them. We were sending them to Ukraine, to the front,” he said.

A WhatsApp chat allowed soldiers to give the volunteers feedback on how their drones performed. Members of the group ended up training other companies in the art of assembling drones, and it became a movement in Lithuania for people to spend part of their weekend helping Ukraine.

People would come into a workshop on a weekend to build them. At first, it took eight hours to make one drone, but the time required reduced to four hours.

“Ukraine is 700km from Vilnius. Every citizen in Lithuania would say the same – they are defending not just Ukraine, they are defending us as well, because if Ukraine falls, we are next,” he said.

For Danielius Uznys, chief of staff at Nord Security, it is natural for Lithuanians to want to support Ukraine. The company has delivered Nord security products to help human rights activists and has made financial donations to help Ukraine defend itself.

As a thank you, Ukraine sent two presentation boxes of soil from the front line in the war, which are on display in Nord Security’s headquarters.

“Ukraine is near us, and in our hearts, so we keep supporting it in different ways. Our staff, some of them on a personal level, are going to Ukraine, delivering stuff, building drones, etc. That goes without saying,” he said.

Meanwhile, Oxylabs has donated funds to a Ukrainian support organisation, which is buying medical equipment and robots to support front-line troops and charities providing support for vulnerable groups.

The company is also providing free access to its web data infrastructure to organisations, including Bellingcat and Debunk.org, and journalists who investigate and disrupt disinformation campaigns put out by Russia and others.
summary:
Cyber City in Lithuania’s capital, Vilnius, was built on the site of a textile factory, which in its day turned out millions of socks a year. Today, you can see reminders of the textile design in the decoration of buildings that house more than 3,000 technology professionals and high-tech businesses.

Since it gained independence from the Soviet Union in 1990, Lithuania, a country of 2.8 million people, has reinvented itself as a home for tech startups. Since 2020, the value of Lithuania’s tech sector has grown by a factor of six to reach €16.4bn. Many of its companies are household names, such as Vinted, the online second-hand fashion market, and cyber security and privacy company Nord Security. 

Lithuania’s technology sector has grown through a mixture of inward investment and homegrown companies.

title: Interview: Virgin Media O2's Dana Haidan on growing a green strategy
raw_text:
For nearly four years, Virgin Media O2 (VMO2) chief sustainability officer (CSO) Dana Haidan has been pushing ahead with environment, social and governance (ESG) strategy at the telecoms provider – and getting real results for her efforts.

Prioritising and embedding sustainability, including emissions reduction, throughout the business is not just a nice-to-have but supports customer trust, as well as demand for its products and services – nurturing 46.4 million mobile connections, 5.5 million consumer fixed-line customers, and total services revenue exceeding £2bn in its year to first-quarter (Q1) 2026.

It all adds up to a reshaping of the entity’s strategy post-merger in 2021. Key vehicles include VMO2’s new Responsible Business and Green Transition plans, built out from an earlier Better Connections initiative. The provider cut Scope 1 and 2 carbon emissions by 16% in 2025 and 63% versus its 2020 baseline.

By 2030, VMO2 promises to have slashed Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 90% versus 2020. Haidan says VMO2 is poised to hit its science-based targets initiative (SBTi) validated net-zero goal across operations, products and supply chain by 2040.

“We’ve set out really ambitious targets for our decarbonisation work, our circularity and digital inclusion,” she says. “In five years, we’ve delivered nearly all of our targets, especially net zero.”

For Haidan, the future is about modernisation that incorporates green energy efficiencies throughout the network estate, reducing carbon per gigabyte (GB) with every network extension and a new generation of network technologies. Another big target is e-waste, she says.

Slashing e-waste can be huge for emissions reduction

Research suggests 52% to 82% of the total carbon footprint of IT hardware, including smartphones, comes from mineral mining, manufacturing and transport through the supply chain. From 2014-2020, Scope 3 emissions from embodied carbon in e-waste increased 53% to 580mt3  of CO2-equivalent (CO2e) emissions, and that is expected to reach 852mt3 in 2030.

Other studies now show that coloured microparticles and nanoparticles swept into the atmosphere absorb and trap heat and contribute to global warming. The same research suggests total carbon emissions could be halved through better design as well as reduction of OEM resource use and circularity (“repair, refurbish, recycle”).

“We’ve been looking at our internal e-waste to manage that, and externally at the wider industry issues of people being open to buying refurbed and recycling. Removing stigma and myths around all of that – including communicating very clearly what happens to their data,” Haidan says. “We want it to become a very viable option and the norm for people to buy second-hand and to recycle their devices.”

VMO2 has committed to removing single-use plastic from all its own-branded customer packaging, as well as doubling the number of consumers buying “high-quality and affordable” second-hand or refurbished devices and the proportion of devices recycled.

VMO2 also founded a kind of food bank for data, with 5,000 data hubs nationally. It’s working with city councils to launch 30 local device recycling banks in 30 cities by 2030, following a successful pilot in Coventry.

Since 2009, the O2 Recycle service has recycled four million devices, paying £356m to consumers, with zero parts going to landfill. VMO2 has repaired, reused and recycled an estimated total 7.5 million bits of customer kit.

“We have scaled up our circularity work, resulting in over 10 million customers buying refurb, recycling their devices and donating their tech,” she says.

More can be achieved by transforming regulation to support circularity, she says. European directives and concepts such as digital passporting for traceability have “helped things along”, but more must be done, including in the UK.

“For example, right now there is no universal grading standard for devices. One reseller will tell you, this is a Grade A, another that it’s Grade B,” she says.

Ultimately, unless they’re more specifically interested in circularity and recycling, customers typically seek a relatively friction-less experience. They want it to be easy to find what they want. That restricts sales of refurbished kit today.

Insurance strategies could provide part of the answer, and VMO2 has been considering how to devise an effective proposition that would give second-hand device purchases more assurance of performance and reliability, she says.

People need peace of mind that goes beyond whether a device can be resold and used again safely. They want to know whether a refurb will perform like new, or close enough, in terms of battery life, camera and screen, for instance.

Giving customers connectivity, control and choices

VMO2 also targets digital inclusion, without which people can’t necessarily invest in the products or solutions they most favour. “Greener” offerings have often come at a higher price point that excludes people who would go green if they could afford it. Others have been hamstrung by poor customer experiences that put people off.

“So, we’ve started to introduce ‘intersectional’ ways of addressing these issues,” Haidan says. “Because there is a segment in society that cannot afford connectivity, we believe we have a role to play in supporting [connectivity]. A lot also don’t have access to devices: domestic violence victims, refugees, asylum seekers.”

That means considering how data and device access can be increased through programmes and incentives, such as discounts or free offers for certain population segments or who meet certain criteria. VMO2’s five-year Community Calling programme re-homed 2025 devices in 2025, many coming from VMO2’s own supply chain.

Digital wellbeing might include almost anything that gives customers control and choice over how they interact with tech – right up to supporting anti-scam artificial intelligence (AI) tools for customer security.

“Ultimately, if we’re not doing our part on these issues, we’ve done something fundamentally wrong as a business”

Dana Haidan, Virgin Media O2

So for VMO2, sustainable, responsible business entails a broader approach, that looks at more value-adding approaches. Key to this is a “double materiality” approach as per the EU’s Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) framework. This directive is to consider the effects of the world on the business as well as the converse, tied to stringent sustainability reporting.

While grateful that UK companies don’t have to comply with all the related minutiae, the double-materiality process is “really useful”, says Haidan, in that it helps centre customer trust and expectations of VMO2 as a brand as well as keeping demand requirements and future developments in scope. She’s especially proud of signing two 10-year UK renewable power purchase agreements.

Perhaps surprisingly, VMO2 doesn’t directly market its products as green. Rather, being environmentally sound should be the norm, says Haidan. Benefits must flow from a carefully designed proposition that incorporates greenness essentially as a byproduct, Haidan confirms. Of course, people don’t like to be preached at, either.

“Let’s face it: most focus first on ‘what’s in it for me’. So, we focus heavily on the fact that when you hand your device in, we pay you for it. But our impact on the environment is a priority, and how we ensure we decarbonise, take care of our carbon footprint and invest in renewable energies, sending a strong market signal.”

VMO2 embeds and infuses sustainability and business responsibility throughout the operation, with carbon dashboarding and targets now embedded in strategy and key performance indicators (KPIs) at key intervention points through the next five years – a key change from the earlier Better Connections plan. Consideration of nature impacts is also expanding, says Haidan.

VMO2’s investment committee examines all capital expenditure (capex) and investments through the lens of “responsible business” KPIs. All suppliers too must meet its “responsible business” criteria, and even employee bonuses are looked at.

“Ultimately, if we’re not doing our part on these issues, we’ve done something fundamentally wrong as a business. They’re not side initiatives,” Haidan says. “None of the targets or ambitions we’ve got in our strategy are negotiable. It’s been quite a shift, because you wouldn’t previously see sustainability strategies looking at the way the business is run, instead of just when you’re doing your ESG disclosures.”

Tough trade-offs drive commensurate rewards

Haidan says that three aspects she loves about her job are it’s growing systemic importance, the depth of impact she’s been able to achieve, and the chance to see the best in people.

Haidan’s sustainability-focused career spans 17 years so far. In 2022, she joined VMO2 after senior sustainability roles at Visa, Vodafone, consultancy Sustainable Square, and QatarGas (now QatarEnergy) on the back of a Carnegie Mellon (CMU) bachelor’s in business administration and post-grad sustainability studies at Oxford and Cambridge.

She grew up between the US and Qatar. “My father worked as an engineer in the fossil fuels industry. Towards the end of his career, he became more aware of the wider impact: it was the Al Gore ‘Inconvenient Truth’ era! That became all he read about and slowly became my obsession too,” Haidan says.

Gore, 1993-2001 US vice-president, won global acclaim for his campaign to educate people about global warming. Beginning in 1989, his crusade peaked with 2006 documentary An Inconvenient Truth – still ranked 20 years later as the 18th biggest-earning documentary since records began.

Haidan knew “very early on” what she wanted to do, although her parents didn’t think much of her career prospects. “Even doubting I’d find a job!” she jokes. “I thought of myself as an activist with a corporate lanyard for the longest time. Not practical. I’ve learned – the hard way – that it can be polarising. Yet people really do want to do the right thing.”

As other CSOs have found, the key is more often about making tough trade-offs and speaking the right language with the right stakeholders. Often, you’ll get better results by focusing on topics such as business resilience, rather than net zero as such.

“We talk about it through the lens of margin protections, giving choice and so changing the language of how you engage,” Haidan says.

Although activism “definitely works” in places, she’s “not so sure” about the corporation. What you do is make a difference, doing great work, while delivering growth and business benefits. The two goals don’t need to compete.

“You can decouple negative environmental impacts from growth. And we’ve done it, and we’re doing it, with conviction at the highest levels of organisation that this is what we need to remain relevant for the long term.”

  • Interview: Hitachi Vantara takes long view on business and sustainability. We talk to Hitachi Vantara’s Simon Ninan about how the company looks to the far horizon when trying to match business needs with those of society.
  • Setting achievable sustainability targets in the age of AI infrastructure. AI demands high-density compute, challenging sustainability goals. CIOs must shift from vague targets to operational, audited metrics and responsible hardware lifecycle management.

Prioritising and embedding sustainability, including emissions reduction, throughout the business is not just a nice-to-have but supports customer trust, as well as demand for its products and services – nurturing 46.4 million mobile connections, 5.5 million consumer fixed-line customers, and total services revenue exceeding £2bn in its year to first-quarter (Q1) 2026.

Key vehicles include VMO2’s new Responsible Business and Green Transition plans, built out from an earlier Better Connections initiative. The provider cut Scope 1 and 2 carbon emissions by 16% in 2025 and 63% versus its 2020 baseline.

By 2030, VMO2 promises to have slashed Scope 1 and 2 emissions by 90% versus 2020. Haidan says VMO2 is poised to hit its science-based targets initiative (SBTi) validated net-zero goal across operations, products and supply chain by 2040.

title: Burnham government to drop digital ID and tech department
raw_text:
Andy Burnham’s premiership is set to start with the scrapping of controversial plans for a digital ID scheme for all UK adults.

The Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) is also likely to be dumped as Keir Starmer ends his time in Number 10.

The digital ID scheme aims to create a national ID to make public services quicker and more secure to access, with the potential to include private sector services, such as gym memberships or parcel collections.

It was first announced by prime minister Keir Starmer in September 2025, but has since been watered down. It was originally planned to be mandatory for right-to-work checks, but in January 2026, the government backtracked on this and removed the compulsory aspect, following uproar from privacy campaigners.

According to reports, a Burnham spokesperson said the ID scheme would be ended to allow the government to redirect resources. “The time and resource that was going to be spent on a national ID scheme will go instead to where it's most needed, such as helping with the cost of living,” the spokesperson was reported as saying.

Just months ago, the King’s Speech indicated Starmer’s government would make a renewed effort in the implementation of the ID plan.

Tech department to be cut

Burnham’s reported plans to ditch DSIT and spread its activities across other departments has come in for criticism from the IT sector. IT industry trade bodies TechUK and the Startup Coalition have written to Burnham describing it as the “wrong change at the wrong moment”.

The letter from TechUK’s CEO Julian David and executive director at Startup Coalition Dom Hallas warned Burnham that scrapping the department will send a “terrible signal to a sector growing at 10% a year”.

“DSIT works because it brings researchers, AI practitioners, innovators and policymakers together in one department – a single front door that gives the tech sector clarity, pace and a government partner that understands technology and champions the sector in the Cabinet,” the incoming Prime Minister was told in the letter.

He was warned that “breaking up the work of DSIT endeavours” such as the AI Safety Institute, the Sovereign AI fund and Government Digital Service “would slow momentum at a time when pace is essential for both the growth of the economy and our standing on the global stage”.

The IT trade bodies said they supported Burnham’s vision to bring growth to every postcode in Britain, but added: “We want you to be able to leverage the tech sector to deliver on your ambitions to change our economy and society for the better. To do so, we should use this moment to strengthen tech leadership at the heart of government, not dismantle it.”

Burnham has already stated that he will continue to fuel the government’s strategy to insource government work, including IT.

According to a Financial Times report, he told a meeting of Labour MPs he would curb outsourcers and bring government contracts in house, putting control into public hands.

Burnham is quoted as criticising “an outsourced state with little accountability” as he spoke to MPs. His desire to bring government contracts in house is already a policy of the Labour government, but his statement to MPs adds urgency as he will be eager to hit the ground running with his stated plan.

Read more about digital ID

  • Eight-week consultation aims to get the public’s view on how the proposed digital ID system would work, and contemplates introducing a universal unique identifier linked to the ID.
  • The Home Affairs Committee hearing on digital ID reveals consultation is due next week; there will be no central database; and while government wants to build the system in-house, it will not replace private digital ID providers.
  • The proposed national digital identity app will no longer be compulsory for conducting right-to-work checks, removing the most contentious and widely criticised element of the scheme.

title: Dutch Surf tests practical route out of Big Tech dependency
raw_text:
When a group of Dutch researchers studying the societal impact of algorithms realised they were doing that work entirely on platforms they had no control over, they turned to Surf, the IT cooperative for Dutch education and research. The answer they got was honest: “we do not have an alternative, but we will figure it out”.

Four years later, that request from AlgoSoc, a research programme across five Dutch universities, has grown into a series of open source pilots across Surf’s 120 member institutions. Wladimir Mufty, programme manager for public value and collective digital sovereignty at Surf. The cooperative, comparable to Jisc in the UK, has spent five years working with a small team on turning abstract values such as privacy and autonomy into concrete technology choices.

“Sovereignty is not binary,” says Mufty. “It is not a switch you flip on or off. But the more control you have, the more of those values you can factor into your decisions.”

In practice, that means running pilots, placing alternatives alongside existing services and scaling up what proves viable. “We do not just write position papers about becoming independent from a supplier,” he adds. “We try to act on them as well.”

The dependency that Surf is trying to address did not appear overnight. For years, the relationship between Dutch education institutions and their technology suppliers was treated as broadly symmetrical: institutions paid, suppliers delivered, and if a supplier underperformed, you could take your business elsewhere. That assumption no longer holds. “When a vendor holds your data, your identity systems and your infrastructure, you cannot simply walk over to the competitor,” says Mufty. “This is a fundamentally different kind of dependency than buying gas or grain.”

The Covid-19 pandemic accelerated the shift. José van Dijck, professor of media and digital society at Utrecht University, has described the pandemic as a turning point that pushed Dutch universities deep into Big Tech dependency. There was an acute need to switch to online education, and commercial platforms could provide ready-made products immediately. Collective services that Surf and universities had built over decades were scaled back in favour of speed.

Mufty sees the same pattern. The risks of depending on a handful of US technology companies were always acknowledged, he says, but treated as theoretical. “Nobody believed a trade war would actually happen, or that a government would cut off access to services overnight,” says Mufty. “That probability was always perceived zero. Now, it’s not zero anymore, and that changes everything.”

From pilot to platform

After AlgoSoc’s request, Surf evaluated several candidates and ended up selecting Nextcloud. “It was different from what people were used to, but it was mature,” says Mufty.

What started as a collaboration environment for a small group of researchers has since expanded. Surfdrive, the cooperative’s file-sharing service used by tens of thousands of people, originally ran on OwnCloud software, but has been migrated to Nextcloud as its back end. Separately, Surf now offers 30 institutions a full Nextcloud workspace including video calls, chat and calendars, used by between 1,500 and 2,000 people.

Other pilots address different dependencies. Surf is testing PeerTube as an alternative video platform, partly because Dutch educational videos about subjects such as drugs or breast cancer research can fall foul of YouTube’s content moderation policies. A Mastodon pilot provides an alternative social media channel. In each case, the ownership of the data, the algorithms and the infrastructure remains with the institutions themselves.

Mufty sees artificial intelligence (AI) creating a new layer of dependency. Institutions face pressure from students and staff to provide the latest tools, while long-term vendor contracts risk deepening the lock-in Surf is trying to loosen elsewhere. The cooperative’s response is an AI hub on its own infrastructure, where open models are offered through a shared environment with high standards and requirements for quality, privacy and legal jurisdiction managed centrally.

“We make sure the hub contains high-quality models,” says Mufty. “If an institution is not satisfied with Mistral, or if one query needs to go to Mistral and another to GPT-NL, it can switch without rebuilding anything.”

For providers, the model creates a different incentive. “You only need to meet our standards once,” he says. “And you potentially have 120 institutions as customers.”

Open source underpins all of these pilot initiatives, but Mufty frames it as an instrument rather than a mission. Of Surf’s 50 services, 38 are built on or with open source software, he says. “Transparency, interoperability, building on each other’s work, reuse: you can achieve those almost exclusively with open source,” says Mufty. “With proprietary software, you pay twice for every improvement you make.”

Proof in a weekend

The clearest evidence of what that means in practice came when Surf had to act on it. Surfdrive ran on OwnCloud for years until changes in the ownership and commercial direction of the company behind it created uncertainty over support contracts and the product’s future.

“Did we enjoy that? No. But did it cause us stress? Also no,” says Mufty. “The code was on our own machines, and we knew alternatives existed.”

After six months of preparation, Surf replaced the OwnCloud foundation with Nextcloud and migrated tens of thousands of users in a single weekend. “If it had been a proprietary system, we could never have moved from one product to another in a weekend,” he adds.

Changing the perception of open source at board level has been a separate effort. For years, the automatic association among education leaders was that open source meant amateurish, insecure and difficult to use. Mufty acknowledges that some projects are not ready for large-scale deployment, but says the conversation has shifted towards what open source can deliver rather than what it is.

Surf and the Dutch Ministry of the Interior have co-organised an open source leadership summit aimed at directors and board members. A second edition is planned for 1 October 2026, expanding to include leaders from water authorities and provincial governments.

Surf is not operating in isolation in the Netherlands. On 10 July, the Dutch Tax Administration paused the roll-out of Microsoft 365 after the government’s IT advisory board concluded that the current approach would not deliver a suitable or future-proof working environment. That same day, five Dutch regulators, the Netherlands Authority for Consumers and Markets, the Dutch Authority for the Financial Markets, the Dutch Data Protection Authority, De Nederlandsche Bank and the Dutch Authority for Digital Infrastructure, published a joint position paper calling on public authorities and businesses to make digital autonomy a central consideration in IT procurement.

Collective control but individual compromise

The cooperative model helps absorb early risk. Surf pools costs and distributes them proportionally across its members, from small vocational colleges to large research universities, so that early adopters do not carry the full burden alone. But Mufty is candid about the tension in that model. To build collective autonomy, each institution must accept less individual control over a shared service.

“Sometimes an institution thinks: I would have preferred this feature, or a little more of that,” he says. “But you cannot choose your own background colour when you are building a platform together.”

For the programme manager, the harder problem is not the absence of alternatives, but the expectation that an alternative must match every feature of an established platform before anyone is willing to use it. Surf itself offers 14 cloud services to its members. However, most institutions still choose the US options.

“The only way to turn something that is a six into an eight is to start using it,” says Mufty. “If we keep asking whether it is already as good as Big Tech, the answer will always be ‘no’. That is the whole point.”

  • Nextcloud Summit in Munich shows that Europe’s sovereignty ambitions meet their real test not in the technology, but in the procurement decisions.
  • European Commission launches package spanning chips, cloud and energy to reduce dependency on US and Chinese supply chains.

title: Viva la devolution – rebalancing power through technology
raw_text:
Andy Burnham has promised to deliver the "biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen" as our new prime minister, with power taken away from Westminster and redistributed to all parts of the UK. This could herald the most significant act of devolution since the creation of the Scottish Parliament, Welsh Parliament, and Northern Ireland Assembly in the late 1990s.

Although those changes helped bring power closer to people in some parts of the UK, they’ve also provoked endless debates about a so-called “postcode lottery”, where the quality, funding, and availability of services varies by geographic location, along with the seemingly eternal “West Lothian question” - where non-English constituency MPs still vote on England-only matters.

Added to this is the growing phenomenon of regulatory friction - the compliance costs for businesses and citizens navigating different rules depending on where they happen to operate or live.

Before rushing into another programme of major constitutional reform, the government should pause to assess where the current settlement is working well, where it falls short, and how it can be improved.

This should include making time to consider the transformative impact of technology, a topic that remains surprisingly absent from devolution debates.

Yet digital technologies and practices are a gamechanger in terms of improving how organisations work. They create new options for devolution that were simply unavailable when the current model was developed in the last century - options that can help to strengthen democratic accountability while reducing duplication and lowering costs.

Rather than simply viewing devolution as a question of redistributing power, policymakers should take the opportunity to rethink the design of our public institutions, combining the benefits of local decision-making with the efficiencies of shared digital capabilities.

Techniques such as value-chain and Wardley mapping, for example, can identify where it’s best to share governance and technical infrastructure at a UK-wide level and where it’s better for devolved authorities to innovate using their unique local context and accountability.

Common infrastructure

The UK government has already started to apply lessons from the past three decades of “digital transformation” initiatives. Its 2025 Blueprint for modern digital government, for example, emphasises a more integrated approach to policymaking and digital implementation across both central and local levels through shared infrastructure, data standards, and once-only principles.

 The government can use this work to improve devolution by committing to: 

  • A national digital infrastructure: extend and adopt Gov.uk and other successful platforms, such as Notify and Pay, as foundational national public infrastructure across the UK, using regional and local white labelling where desired. A UK-wide approach to digital identity - building on the success of the Digital Verification Services (DVS) trust framework - could identify and authenticate users regardless of whether they're accessing reserved or devolved services, reducing the need for multiple logins and accounts, lowering costs, and improving the user experience.
  • A secure data access and exchange layer: draw on the experiences of systems such as Estonia’s X-Road and the UK government’s own former secure digital backbone. Estonia achieves near-100% digital public services via decentralised databases - each organisation controls its own data - linked by a secure, logged interoperability layer. Citizens update information once, such as their address, and it propagates with consent and audit trails. Importantly, citizens can also hold officials to account for the use or misuse of their data. A UK version would securely connect central government, devolved administrations, local authorities, and NHS systems while respecting devolved data sovereignty and privacy legislation and rules. It would deliver streamlined cross-tier services, such as health records and benefits, and policy insights without the drawbacks of overly centralised or overly siloed systems.
  • Shared cloud services: expand the use of standardised, commodity shared services - cloud services for the likes of HR, finance, payroll - across all public sector bodies, fixing the failures of the current broken approach, and pool procurement to secure economies of scale and reduce costs and vendor lock-in.
  • Open standards, APIs, and the National Data Library: mandate open standards, APIs (interfaces), and the National Data Library for reusable, high-quality data. This will provide "once-only" acquisition and re-use, with safeguards and resilience, and reduce today’s currently endlessly repeated data collection, helping remove the need for most of the 10,000-plus online forms that pointlessly replicate their paper predecessors. Artificial intelligence (AI) and other digital tools can help analyse government-held data to improve policymaking and resource allocation whilst ensuring rigorous, and transparent, performance and ethical safeguards.

The decision to abolish NHS England demonstrates that the government already recognises the need for significant structural improvements in the public sector to reduce duplication, strengthen democratic accountability, and improve effectiveness and efficiency. It should review the outcome of this major change - what works and what doesn’t - to inform decisions about similar structural improvements elsewhere.

Useful insights can also be gained from work that’s already taken place to rationalise how local government operates, such as the Greater Manchester Combined Authority, which has shown benefits from consolidated technology estates and the better use of data. However, in other areas, such as the failed tri-borough experiment in London, the results have proved less convincing.

Steve Reed, the secretary of state for housing, communities and local government, has also recently confirmed the biggest reorganisation of English local authorities in half a century, with 134 councils streamlined into 38 unitary authorities. Reed says reorganisation will leave councils “ready for devolution”. Whether it leaves them ready in digital terms is another question. And this move to overhaul local government has previously been reported to cost as much as £1.5bn, raising questions about how “machinery of government” changes are being planned and designed at both a political and technological level. The transition support announced, of just over £1m per new unitary authority, is a fraction of what it will cost to converge the systems, processes, contracts, and data estates of the hundreds of councils being abolished, as previous unitarisations in Buckinghamshire, Cumbria, and North Yorkshire showed.

The reorganisation announced by Reed gives new councils barely a year post-election to merge by April 2028. This ambitious timing is another reason why it makes sense to capitalise on the adoption of common platforms and shared infrastructure and services, instead of the new bodies each specifying, procuring, or building their own local solutions yet again - which would be a once-in-50-years design opportunity missed.

An increase in devolution will be judged a success only if it supports more effective, evidence-based policymaking; boosts economic growth and prosperity; streamlines and reduces the duplication of organisations, processes, systems, resources, and costs; lowers the administrative burden ("time tax") on citizens and businesses; frees up more resources for frontline services; and, most importantly, restores public trust.

However, if it becomes just another headline-grabbing political soundbite that ends up rearranging bureaucratic deck chairs while consuming ever more precious resources, it will further undermine the entire concept.

A digital-era operating model

A new phase of devolution is an ideal opportunity for the government to better integrate policymaking and digital expertise to improve how the public sector plans and operates. Among the alternative options it should consider are:

  • Digital bill tagging and gated voting: bills and statutory instruments could be classified by territorial or “community-of-interest” scope based on content, metadata, and impact assessments. A secure parliamentary platform could help to assign and authorise appropriate voting rights, with hybrid and remote digital voting making it cheaper and seamless – for example, all MPs engaged on UK-wide matters; only English MPs vote on England-only matters (addressing the “West Lothian question”); Scottish MPs on Scottish-only matters. This would create a well-mapped "soft" democratic separation of place-based powers without the need for additional physical institutions and all their related overheads
  • Virtual/hybrid devolved chambers: Scottish, Welsh, Northern Irish, English, London or any other agreed constitutional grouping of MPs – such as “the North of England”, “Island Communities”, “Rural Communities”, “Cities” - could convene in dedicated digital sessions or virtual chambers for devolved legislation, scrutiny, and committees. Advanced tools would support real-time transcription and summarisation, secure collaboration, translation if/when needed, and automatic release in real-time onto public websites for transparency and accountability. This would reduce the need for separate physical parliamentary estates long-term while preserving the essential democratic focus on devolved issues. MPs could still choose to sit in an appropriate geographical physical assembly building as and when required - Edinburgh, Cardiff, Belfast, London, etc - to ensure they’re routinely accessible, visible, and accountable to their electorate. But one benefit of a virtual/hybrid approach is that devolution need no longer be defined or limited purely by place or geography
  • Single election layer with commodity digital support: a constituency MP would handle both reserved and devolved matters, removing the need for separate elections for separate assemblies with all their added costs, bureaucracies, complexity, and potential voter fatigue alongside blurred lines of democratic responsibility and accountability. Better technology would help MPs manage their workloads, including tailored policy research, constituency casework, and impact modelling
  • Broader efficiencies: shared parliamentary digital infrastructure - communications and collaboration, document management, Hansard-style systems, petition platforms - would support work across all levels, with common digital repositories/libraries for transparent funding transfers and budgeting, and improved digital participation tools for consultations and scrutiny that work within and across tiers

Separate legislatures incur additional costs without necessarily improving democratic accountability or delivering better outcomes. They consume over £350m a year in direct costs plus substantial hidden costs in terms of their duplicated resources, systems, and corporate functions - money that could instead be flowing into improved frontline services or reducing the administrative burden.

An improved devolutionary settlement, however, provides the opportunity to deliver the upside of enhanced democratic representation and accountability while also yielding major efficiencies.

Some challenges to consider

The political desire to find a better approach already exists, with the secretary of state Reed commenting that, “We won’t achieve effective devolution … with outdated and misaligned structures that slow down delivery, fragmenting public services [and] slowing down important decision making.”

However, improving the UK’s devolutionary model is a major undertaking and will require a cross-party, cross-UK consensus on how best to make it happen. Among the issues to consider are:

  • Political and constitutional buy-in: some politicians and voters may value distinct institutions regardless of their cost and impact on the public purse; integration must preserve autonomy and not feel like recentralisation while also delivering better value for money. Any changes should be supported by legislation and consent.
  • Privacy, security, and trust: robust safeguards, audit logs, and devolved control over data are essential. This should include defining what “digital sovereignty” means not only at the UK level but also in the context of devolved powers and responsibilities. UK cyber security and resilience, for example, must never be diluted by the creation or adoption of lower standards within any constituent part of the UK.
  • Asymmetry: England’s fragmented local devolution, with mayors, combined authorities, local authorities - 32 boroughs in London alone despite the existence of the Greater London Authority - and the lack of an English assembly, also needs revisiting, as the recent government announcements in part acknowledge, to improve accountability and reduce duplication, benefitting from the use of common capabilities and platforms.
  • Implementation: start with well-scoped trials - such as secure data access or DVS-accredited digital identity in specific policy areas - in a real-life environment with real people involved, built on cloud-based platforms and other common infrastructure, and use intergovernmental agreements for devolved participation, using an incremental test, learn, and grow approach.

Conclusion

More devolution could be a good thing for the UK, but only if it’s designed to avoid increased fragmentation, duplicated direct costs and overheads, and a growing administrative burden.

Creating yet more layers of bureaucracy on top of an already complex system could, however, have the very opposite impact - further alienating an already cynical public and directing more precious resources away from where they’re most needed and can have the most beneficial impact.

A better devolution settlement can build on what already works while also fitting the UK’s unique constitutional structure. It can make democratic governance more effective, responsive, and cost-efficient without sacrificing local autonomy and innovation, strengthening both local power and UK cohesion.

Designing, agreeing, and implementing a better approach will require cross-party and intergovernmental collaboration. The good news is that recent digital strategies already point us in a better direction.

By accelerating and extending this work, the government has a perfect opportunity to deliver a democratic digital devolution, with substantial benefits to citizens, businesses, and politicians alike.

James Findlay is a former CTO/CIO in the UK government. He is the author of the Isle of Wight Living Lab proposal, which advocates for the island's designation as a Special Policy Zone for cross-departmental government trials.

Jerry Fishenden is a seasoned CTO/CIO and author of Fracture: The collision between technology and democracy - and how we fix it.
summary:
Andy Burnham has promised to deliver the "biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen" as our new prime minister, with power taken away from Westminster and redistributed to all parts of the UK. This could herald the most significant act of devolution since the creation of the Scottish Parliament, Welsh Parliament, and Northern Ireland Assembly in the late 1990s.

Although those changes helped bring power closer to people in some parts of the UK, they’ve also provoked endless debates about a so-called “postcode lottery”, where the quality, funding, and availability of services varies by geographic location, along with the seemingly eternal “West Lothian question” - where non-English constituency MPs still vote on England-only matters.

Added to this is the growing phenomenon of regulatory friction - the compliance costs for businesses and citizens navigating different rules depending on where they happen to operate or live.

Before rushing into another programme of major constitutional reform, the government should pause to assess where the current settlement is working well, where it falls short, and how it can be improved.

This should include making time to consider the transformative impact of technology, a topic that remains surprisingly absent from devolution debates.

Yet digital technologies and practices are a gamechanger in terms of improving how organisations work. They create new options for devolution that were simply unavailable when the current model was developed in the last century - options that can help to strengthen democratic accountability while reducing duplication and lowering costs.

Rather than simply viewing devolution as a question of redistributing power, policymakers should take the opportunity to rethink the design of our public institutions, combining the benefits of local decision-making with the efficiencies of shared digital capabilities.

Techniques such as value-chain and Wardley mapping, for example, can identify where it’s best to share governance and technical infrastructure at a UK-wide level and where it’s better for devolved authorities to innovate using their unique local context and accountability.

Common infrastructure

The UK government has already started to apply lessons from the past three decades of “digital transformation” initiatives. Its 2025 Blueprint for modern digital government, for example, emphasises a more integrated approach to policymaking and digital implementation across both central and local levels through shared infrastructure, data standards, and once-only principles.

 The government can use this work to improve devolution by committing to: 

  • A national digital infrastructure: extend and adopt Gov.uk and other successful platforms, such as Notify and Pay, as foundational national public infrastructure across the UK, using regional and local white labelling where desired.

title: Where AI fits in modern supply chains
raw_text:
In retail and in manufacturing, artificial intelligence (AI) is being put at the heart of back-office operations to flex stock up and down based on demand and to manage supply chain disruption. But many supply chain decision-makers do not see AI as their main priority.

Boston Consulting Group’s inaugural report on the state of supply chain planning, published in February this year, found that volatility and geopolitical disruption dominate the external planning challenges organisations face. In the BCG Annual state of supply chain planning survey 2025, 70% of respondents in a poll of 180 supply chain decision-makers said that demand volatility is their biggest external planning challenge. Global trade and geopolitics is the main external planning challenge for 64% of the people surveyed, while 62% said their biggest planning challenge is supply volatility.

By a long shot, their biggest internal challenge is forecast inaccuracy and misalignment (78%). End-to-end visibility, which is a significant factor for supply chain decision-makers, is a major concern for 38% of the people polled, while AI is only regarded as a top challenge by 14% of respondents. Yet AI is getting a lot of attention, especially in areas such as forecasting.

Forrester vice-president and principal analyst George Lawrie says machine learning is being used in supply chain forecasting to incorporate multiple variables – such as weather, interest rates and housing stats – in demand forecasting models. He says that while traditional methods relied on time-series data, AI enables the inclusion of additional signals for more accurate predictions. In Lawrie’s experience, AI can also assist in sales and operations planning (SNOP) as it can be used to gather and harmonise data from various stakeholders, such as sales, transportation and finance teams.

Managing contracts is another task well-suited to AI. Lawrie says that generative AI (GenAI) can be used for creating and distributing requests for information (RFIs). “It can generate the RFI. This is very tedious stuff; it can distribute the RFI to carriers or suppliers and AI can also help you to evaluate the responses.”

In Lawrie’s experience, organisations use agentic AI to run through the responses received from suppliers from the RFI, by prioritising and evaluating them. 

Managing suppliers with AI’s help

RWS Global, which runs and manages live events for its clients, has contracts with thousands of performers, and has been using new AI features built into Box to automate workflows such as the contract approval workflow, saving many hours in terms of manual processing.

When Computer Weekly interviewed Jake McCoy, chief operating officer at RWS Global, in February, he said the automation of the contract approval workflow saves many hours in terms of manual processing: “The end user types in the information that needs to go into a contract via Box Doc Gen, which is then sent over to legal [department] for approval.” 

Once approved, the contract is sent out automatically and signed using Box Sign. The signed contract is then uploaded to the cloud. The end-to-end workflow has meant that RWS Global’s contract processing time has been reduced from 20 minutes to under two minutes per contract, reducing what once took more than 8.5 workdays for 200 hires to just five hours.

Using AI with digital twin for troubleshooting

One company that has been using AI  in its supply chain is consumer packaged goods giant Unilever, which has been working with Accenture to deploy AI and digital twins across its global manufacturing network. Unilever says it is able to predict 95% of process flow restrictions in deodorant stick manufacturing, delivering a 20% reduction in waste and a 10% uplift in capacity.

Discussing how the AI works with digital twins, Vicky Cuthbert, chief product supply chain officer of personal care at Unilever, says: “At our Raeford site in North Carolina, the digital twin sits on top of existing production systems, using AI to monitor what’s happening in real time. It tracks key process parameters, such as temperature, flow and pressure, to quickly flag any abnormalities that could affect quality or slow down production. As it can spot issues early, teams can identify root causes much faster, reducing time spent troubleshooting.”

If an abnormality is detected, the system proactively alerts line teams and ensures a consistent escalation process. Cuthbert says the digital twin provides predictive insights beyond fixed rule-based thresholds. “By analysing multiple factors at once, the digital twin can detect more complex and hidden patterns that wouldn’t normally be visible, helping predict issues before they occur. It also gives teams better visibility across production by tracking batch-level performance, including batch size and flow, helping to optimise throughput,” she says.

From an operational standpoint, the digital twin integrates with existing factory systems, enhancing current automation rather than replacing it. Unilever’s plan is to move towards a more closed-loop system, where AI agents can increasingly recommend decisions autonomously, with human oversight.

AI for more effective retail planning

On the retail side, last year, Debenhams Group – home to brands including PrettyLittleThing, Boohoo, BoohooMAN and Karen Millen – announced it has been working with Peak, the AI platform from UiPath, to roll out intelligent automation, which is providing the retailer with real-time, automated pricing for thousands of products.

According to Debenhams Group, the AI system helps it to respond more effectively to changing demand, seasonal trends and inventory levels. The system is being used to protect margins, minimise excess stock and deliver more competitive pricing to customers.

Debenhams CEO Dan Finley sees AI as a technology that is transforming how the retailer manages stock. He says: “We’re embracing AI to make smarter, faster decisions that simplify our operations and enhance the customer experience. This technology will transform how we manage stock and pricing – especially during the busy festive season – and help us to continue to deliver great value and service across all our brands.”

Debenhams also expanded its partnership with Amazon Web Services (AWS), adding in the cloud provider’s AI tools. It has used AWS’s serverless cloud technology as the platform on which it has developed a marketplace model to facilitate the faster onboarding of third-party sellers. Debenhams says the marketplace model also provides a broader product selection and helps to simplify the purchasing journey for its customers

Among the uses of AI at Debenhams is AI-generated content powered by Amazon Bedrock, which automates product descriptions and translations across tens of thousands of products. According to AWS, the technology also translates these descriptions into six languages automatically, which means products are ready to sell in different countries quicker.

AI is finding a place in the supply chain

What these examples show is that across retail, manufacturing and other sectors, AI is being used in areas such as onboarding suppliers and helping to streamline supply chain operations. Undoubtedly, as businesses become more confident in using agentic AI, there will be plenty of role to deploy such systems to manage supply chains more effectively.

Yet while there is a lot of interest in AI, according to BCG, fully autonomous planning remains an aspiration. “Most value today comes from foundational applications – improving forecasting, exception management, data interpretation and workflow automation – rather than from lights-out planning,” the authors of the Why AI alone isn’t enough report noted.

According to BCG, organisations that attempt to leapfrog through the process of planning maturity by means of AI alone tend to struggle, while those that layer AI deliberately onto stable planning foundations see more durable gains. In other words, like other areas of business operations, AI will not fix a broken process. But as Forrester Lawrie notes, it can streamline sales and operations and GenAI can be applied to speed up laborious processes such as preparing and managing RFIs.

Read more stories about AI in supply chain management

  • GenAI is ready for more autonomy, but supply chains are not: Disruptions, vulnerabilities, late deliveries and customer interactions are among the supply chain concerns that GenAI could address autonomously – if businesses can trust it.
  • How ERP and supply chain platforms protect profits: Working capital, forecast accuracy and supplier exposure are governed as much by ERP and supply chain design as finance policy, making supply chain platforms key to margin control.

In retail and in manufacturing, artificial intelligence (AI) is being put at the heart of back-office operations to flex stock up and down based on demand and to manage supply chain disruption. But many supply chain decision-makers do not see AI as their main priority.

Boston Consulting Group’s inaugural report on the state of supply chain planning, published in February this year, found that volatility and geopolitical disruption dominate the external planning challenges organisations face. In the BCG Annual state of supply chain planning survey 2025, 70% of respondents in a poll of 180 supply chain decision-makers said that demand volatility is their biggest external planning challenge. Global trade and geopolitics is the main external planning challenge for 64% of the people surveyed, while 62% said their biggest planning challenge is supply volatility.

By a long shot, their biggest internal challenge is forecast inaccuracy and misalignment (78%).

title: CW@60: The real promise of technology is human
raw_text:
On 22 September 2026, Computer Weekly turns 60. To mark the milestone, we asked some of our friends - experts, parliamentarians, IT leaders and suppliers - for their perspectives on how tech has changed their lives over six decades. What's changed the most for you since then? 

I still remember the moment I realised artificial intelligence (AI) had turned a corner. It was no longer something we talked about in strategy sessions or boardrooms. It was already at work, reshaping decisions, risks, and outcomes in real time.

I was visiting a financial institution in Asia that had deployed machine learning across its transaction network. What I saw was not automation in the traditional sense. Not software executing a fixed set of rules. The system was learning.

It was identifying fraud patterns in milliseconds, adapting to new threats without waiting to be told what to look for, and protecting customers at a scale no human team could match. That was the moment it became clear to me that technology had moved beyond a support role. In this new era, it is the engine.

Hidden infrastructure

I studied electrical engineering at Rutgers University because I was fascinated by how systems work and how the unseen architecture beneath the surface creates momentum, resilience, and change. Even then, I was drawn to the systems people rarely notice but depend on every day - the hidden infrastructure that makes everything else possible.

I joined Dell Technologies in 1999, at a moment when the world was steeling itself for Y2K – it was an extraordinary time to be entering the tech industry. Looking back over the past 25 years, I’ve seen technology reshape businesses and our daily lives in ways that have been inspiring, fascinating, awe-inspiring, and at times even shocking.

I’ve had a front-row seat to one of the most consequential transformations in modern business, and among the most remarkable has been the rise of Asia-Pacific as one of the world’s most dynamic digital regions. Yet even after decades in this industry, nothing has prepared me for the sheer pace of change we’re witnessing now.

We used to talk about a globally connected society in theoretical terms. But today, most of us carry more computing power in our pocket than entire datacentres once held. The distance between an idea and its impact has narrowed dramatically. Technology does not move in decades anymore. In many sectors, it barely moves in quarters.

Time compression

I feel that compression of time in my own work and life. Connectivity lets me stay productive even on a plane; emails and texts reach people instantly, so decisions don't wait; and online learning keeps me ahead on emerging industry trends and the business benefits behind them, wherever and whenever. These seem like small conveniences on their own, but together have been a transformative change in the way we work and how much we can accomplish in a day.

The change reaches beyond productivity. When my wife and I moved overseas, we took real comfort in knowing that if a medical emergency arose, our doctors across geographical borders would have immediate access to our digital health records. Technology doing something deeply human – giving people peace of mind.

The conversations I have with leaders across the region go far beyond whether AI matters. We are exploring how we can adopt it responsibly, scale it securely, and ensure progress does not deepen existing divides. These conversations have revealed three common questions:

  • How do we manage the sheer volume of data we now generate?
  • How do we integrate intelligent systems without compromising security, trust, or accountability?
  • And how do we ensure the benefits of technological change reach people and communities fairly and equitably?

Those may sound like technical questions, but all point to desired human outcomes.

People matter most

We are all still early in our journeys with AI. For instance, the CRIT - which stands for context, role, interview and task - framework of optimising AI interactions has helped me in my daily work, such as preparing for meetings or guiding market research. It’s a modest start, but it clearly shows how these tools can sharpen our thinking and free us to focus on what matters most - the people we are speaking to, sitting on the other side of the table.

I have seen the same principle at work across our region. In healthcare, better infrastructure is speeding up research. In education, connectivity is extending access to education to students in remote villages far beyond major cities.

Enterprise computing’s history has taught us the same lesson time and again – technology is impressive, but the outcome on society is what stays with you.

As AI becomes embedded in how we work, govern, build, and live, our responsibility is to keep that human outcome firmly at the centre. The next chapter of technology will not be judged simply by whether systems become faster or models become smarter. It will be judged by whether innovation expands opportunity, strengthens trust, and improves lives.

If I could say one thing to my younger self, it would be this - the real promise of technology is the human potential. That is the standard we must hold ourselves to, and the only measure of success that will matter.  

Richard McLaughlin is president of Asia-Pacific, Japan and Greater China at Dell Technologies.

raw_text: HONG KONG, July 21, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- ATFX has officially launched the World Trading Cup under the campaign theme “Fight for Your Region. title: Century Aluminum Applauds President Trump’s Action to Spur Domestic Aluminum Production
raw_text: CHICAGO, July 20, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Century Aluminum Company (NASDAQ: CENX) applauds President Donald Trump’s latest executive order to incentivize investments that make American manufacturing great again. Signed today, the executive order allows approved companies investing in new primary aluminum production in the United States to import a commensurate amount of primary aluminum at a reduced tariff rate. The new tariff rate for such companies will now be half the otherwise applicable rate for such imports.
